title: WiFi Security Protocols
description: Authentication and encryption standards that protect wireless networks from unauthorized access.
fullExplanation: WiFi security has evolved from the broken WEP (1997) through WPA and WPA2 to the current WPA3 standard. WEP used RC4 with static keys and was cracked in minutes. WPA introduced TKIP as a stopgap. WPA2 with AES-CCMP became the standard for over a decade. WPA3 (2018) introduced SAE (Simultaneous Authentication of Equals) replacing the vulnerable PSK handshake, and mandates 192-bit encryption for enterprise networks.

protocols.name: WEP
protocols.weakness: IV reuse, weak RC4 — cracked in <60 seconds

protocols.name: WPA2
protocols.weakness: KRACK attack, offline dictionary attacks on PSK

modes.description: Pre-Shared Key — single password for all users. Home/small office use.
Each user authenticates with individual credentials via RADIUS server. Corporate use.
Simultaneous Authentication of Equals — replaces PSK. Resistant to offline dictionary attacks.
Opportunistic Wireless Encryption — encrypts open networks without password.

keyFacts: WEP: cracked in under 60 seconds with aircrack-ng — never use
WPA2-PSK: vulnerable to offline dictionary attacks if weak password
KRACK (2017): WPA2 key reinstallation attack — patched in most devices
WPA3-SAE: forward secrecy — past traffic safe even if password later compromised
802.1X: each user gets unique session key — compromise of one user doesn't affect others
PMKID attack: crack WPA2 without capturing 4-way handshake
Hidden SSID: no security benefit — easily discovered with passive scanning
